title: Anderson Localization: A Floquet operator Krylov space perspective
abstract: The problem of Anderson localization, as well as the single particle localization-delocalizaton transition of the Aubry-André model, is studied employing operator Krylov space methods. It is shown that even when the dynamics is generated by a Hamiltonian, studying the dynamics at stroboscopic rather than continuous times has its advantages. In particular, mapping the dynamics to an effective Floquet problem results in an operator Krylov space description where quantities such as the spectral function can be computed with fewer computational resources, while a moment method exists that allows for the extraction of Krylov parameters directly from the discrete time autocorrelation function. For stroboscopic dynamics, the operator Krylov space corresponds to the dynamics of an edge operator of an inhomogeneous Floquet transverse field Ising model, with the parameters of this effective model generated recursively. The Krylov parameters show disorder-averaged renormalization with their distribution narrowing as the recursion step increases. It is shown that a more physical spectral function is obtained from the Krylov parameters obtained from the disorder-averaged autocorrelation function, rather than the disorder-averaged Krylov parameters. The delocalized (localized) phase is shown to correspond to the appearance (absence) of a Porter-Thomas distribution, a ballistically propagating (localized) wavefront in operator Krylov space, and a smooth (discrete) Berstein-Szegö power-spectrum. The localization-delocalization transition is also demonstrated in operator Krylov space. A Porter-Thomas distribution is also observed at the critical point. The long-time dynamics and the inverse participation ratio at the critical point is shown to exhibit behavior consistent with a multi-fractal scaling with system size.
summary: This paper develops a Krylov space-based approach to study Anderson localization by treating continuous-time dynamics as a discrete-time Floquet problem. It introduces an efficient moment method for computing spectral functions and identifies clear numerical signatures for the localization-delocalization transition. The work provides a powerful tool for analyzing the long-time dynamics of disordered and quasi-periodic systems.
main_problem: Investigating Anderson localization and the localization-delocalization transition in the Aubry-Andre model using an operator Krylov space perspective.
main_result: The study demonstrates that mapping continuous-time dynamics to a stroboscopic Floquet problem allows for efficient spectral reconstruction and identifies specific signatures of localization, such as the absence of a Porter-Thomas distribution and power-law decay of Krylov angles.
method: The authors employ operator Krylov space methods, specifically the Arnoldi iteration and a more efficient 'moment method' to extract Krylov parameters from the discrete-time autocorrelation function.
model_or_system: The primary models are the 1D Anderson model (disordered) and the Aubary-Andre model (quasi-periodic), mapped to an effective 1D Floquet transverse field Ising model.
key_observables: Autocorrelation function, Krylov angles (theta_j), spectral function, Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), and Porter-Thomas distribution.
important_parameters: Disorder strength (h), stroboscopic time step (T), and system size (L).
main_assumptions: The study focuses on non-interacting fermionic systems and assumes the validity of the stroboscopic approximation for studying long-time dynamics.
figures_summary: Figures show phase diagrams of the Floquet Ising model, disorder-averaged autocorrelation saturation, power-law decay of Krylov angles, and comparisons of different spectral reconstruction schemes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Krylov space mapping, compares numerical algorithms (Gram-Schmidt vs. Moment method), analyzes the localization-delocalization transition in the Anderson and Aubry-Andre models, and discusses the statistical properties of Krylov parameters and spectral functions.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it provides a new numerical framework for studying operator spreading and spectral properties in Floquet systems, which is a central theme in studying periodically driven quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Results for the Anderson model for L = 200 and T = 1.5, with disorder strengths h = 1, 2, 5, 10, averaged over 15,000 realizations. Top panels (x-axes are on a log-scale): the disorder-averaged autocorrelation saturates to a constant value at late times (left panel). The relative standard deviation of the autocorrelation also saturates at late times (middle panel). The disorder averaged cosine of the Krylov angles is shown in the right panel, where the odd/even angles approach π/2 from above/below. Bottom panels: log–log plots of the averaged cosines of the odd (left panel) and even (middle panel) Krylov angles. For strong disorder, ⟨cos θ2j−1⟩∼1/√j, while |⟨cos θ2j⟩| ∼1/j for all...

FIG. 2. The Krylov angles are computed from the disorder-averaged autocorrelation (upper left panel of Fig. 1) and shown in the left panel. In contrast to the results in Fig. 1, both the odd and even Krylov angles exhibit the same asymptotic power-law behavior, | cos θj| ∼1/j for large j (middle panel). Consequently, the trajectory of the Krylov angles in the phase diagram (right panel) lies along the diagonal. These results are consistent with the analytic solution for a constant autocorrelation which suggests a power-law localized 0-mode in Krylov space [10].

FIG. 3. Approximate spectral functions for the Anderson model obtained from different approximation schemes for L = 200, T = 1.5, h = 10. Top panels: Bernstein–Szeg˝o approximation (13) applied to a single realization (left panel) and to the average over 15,000 realizations of the cosine of the Krylov angles (right panel). Bottom panels: Approximate spectral function obtained from the Bernstein–Szeg˝o approximation (13) based on Krylov angles extracted from the disorder-averaged autocorrelation (left panel) ; truncated Fourier transform (16) computed from the disorder-averaged autocorrelation is evaluated only at (2n∗+ 1) discrete values of the frequencies: ω = 2mπ/(2n∗+ 1) with m = −n∗,...

FIG. 4. Comparison of different methods for extracting the disorder-averaged spectral function. The bottom two panels of Fig. 3, corresponding to the Bernstein–Szeg˝o approxima- tion with k∗= 40 (Φ40) and the truncated Fourier transform with n∗= 40 (Φ(40)), are plotted together with the results obtained from the Lehmann representation (34), the latter averaged over 5000 realizations. All three methods show good agreement, while the Bernstein–Szeg˝o approximation more ef- ficiently captures the delta-function peak at ω = 0.

FIG. 5. Delocalized phase of the Aubry–Andr´e model with h = 1, T = 10, and system sizes L = 100, 200, 400. Top panels: autocorrelation (left), and the corresponding Krylov angles (right). Bottom panels: PDF of the autocorrelation (left) and the corresponding CDF (right). Both the PDF and CDF are computed from the first 200 time steps of the autocorrelation.
